name: Poludnica
description: Poludnica, also known as Południca, Noon Witch, or Lady Midday, is a figure in Slavic mythology, typically depicted as a female demon or spirit associated with the heat of noontime, especially during the harvest season. She is believed to appear in fields during the hottest part of the day, often as a beautiful, young woman dressed in white, a ragged old woman, or even a whirlwind of dust. Her primary function is to torment field workers, particularly those who work during the sacred midday hours or who neglect their duties. She is said to cause heatstroke, exhaustion, and even madness. In some regions, she is described as carrying a scythe or shears, which she uses to cut off the heads of those who displease her. Poludnica's origins may stem from the agricultural anxieties of early Slavic societies, representing the dangers of the sun and the importance of respecting the natural rhythms of the land. She serves as a cautionary figure, reminding people to rest during the hottest hours and to maintain a balance between work and respite. While primarily a malevolent figure, some interpretations suggest she can also be appeased through offerings or respectful behavior. Her influence extends beyond the fields, as she is sometimes associated with children who misbehave or wander off during the day. The Poludnica embodies the harsh realities of agricultural life and the potential consequences of ignoring the power of nature.
subtitle: Noon Witch of the Fields

keyMyths.title: The Noon Witch's Scythe
keyMyths.description: A farmer, ignoring the midday heat, continued to work. Poludnica appeared and, displeased by his disrespect, cut off his head with her scythe.

keyMyths.title: The Dizziness of Noon
keyMyths.description: A young woman, working in the fields, felt dizzy and disoriented at noon. Villagers believed it was the Poludnica's influence, warning her to rest during the hottest hours.

keyMyths.title: The White Dress in the Fields
keyMyths.description: A traveler saw a woman in a white dress beckoning him into the field at noon. He was warned that it was Poludnica and to avoid her at all costs, lest she lead him to madness.
